title: Refinements to the Solar Polar Magnetic Flux: Implications from Inversion Methodologies
comment: Accepted for publication in AAS Journals
summary: The magnetic fields in the solar polar region are important to our understanding of the internal dynamo process, the global coronal structure, and the origin of the solar wind. The inference of polar fields based on spectropolarimetric observation is highly model-dependent and can suffer from various systematic effects. Here we analyze a raster map of the southern polar region taken by the Hinode Spectro-Polarimeter, utilizing the Stokes Inversion based on Response functions code. The inversions provide height-dependent vector magnetic field maps between optical depths $\log_{10}τ= -2$ and $0$. We examine the impact on the total magnetic flux estimate from adopting (1) 1- vs 2-component atmospheric models via a "filling factor" parameter and (2) different analysis schemes. At $\log_{10}τ= -1.5$, the polar magnetic flux is estimated to be $(1.84 \pm 0.03) \times 10^{21}$ Mx and $(1.38 \pm 0.02) \times 10^{21}$ Mx under the 1- and 2-component atmosphere assumption, respectively. The magnetic flux is approximately constant or increases slightly with height, respectively. We find that the 2-component (1-component) configuration is preferred for 58.3% (32.3%) of the pixels. Different initial guesses, including the input atmosphere model and the filling factor, as well as different inversion settings, can significantly affect the results, especially for locations with weaker polarization signals. Our work highlights the importance of including unresolved magnetic structures or stray light into consideration. Model degeneracy and the convergence to local minima limit the precision of the polar magnetic flux inference (no better than several tens of percent in this case). Higher-resolution observations and advanced inversion and disambiguation algorithms may alleviate these limitations.

AI.tldr: Analysis of solar polar magnetic fields using Hinode spectropolarimetric data reveals significant model-dependence in flux estimates, with 1- vs 2-component atmospheric models yielding different results and highlighting limitations in precision due to unresolved structures and model degeneracy.
AI.motivation: Solar polar magnetic fields are crucial for understanding the solar dynamo, coronal structure, and solar wind origins, but current inference methods are highly model-dependent and susceptible to systematic errors.
AI.method: Analysis of Hinode Spectro-Polarimeter raster maps of the southern polar region using Stokes Inversion based on Response functions (SIR) code to obtain height-dependent vector magnetic field maps between optical depths log₁₀τ = -2 to 0.
AI.result: At log₁₀τ = -1.5, polar magnetic flux estimates are (1.84 ± 0.03) × 10²¹ Mx (1-component) vs (1.38 ± 0.02) × 10²¹ Mx (2-component). 2-component configuration preferred for 58.3% of pixels vs 32.3% for 1-component. Model assumptions significantly affect results, especially for weak polarization signals.
AI.conclusion: Polar magnetic flux inference is limited by model degeneracy and convergence to local minima, with precision no better than several tens of percent. Inclusion of unresolved magnetic structures/stray light is crucial. Higher-resolution observations and advanced inversion/disambiguation algorithms are needed to overcome these limitations.

title: Spatial and spectral constraints on resolved mass-loss of the massive Post-RSG star IRAS 17163-3907 and its Fried Egg Nebula
comment: Accepted for publication in A&A (27 pages including 8 pages appendix, 17 figures, 5 tables)
summary: The fate of massive stars during the latest stages of their evolution is highly dependent on their mass-loss rate and geometry. These processes have a significant influence on stars with masses between 25 and 40 Msun, i.e., type II SN progenitors. We aim to investigate the mass-loss history, geometry, and physical conditions of the yellow hypergiant in a post-RSG stage, IRAS 17163-3907. We place it in context with another famous yellow hypergiant, IRC+10420. We combine M-band high-resolution CRIRES+ spectroscopy with VLTI/MATISSE mid-infrared L-band interferometry, and FORS2 optical spectropolarimetry to probe both the small-scale circumstellar structure and the large-scale dusty environment of IRAS 17163. The CRIRES+ spectrum provides the first M-band coverage of IRAS 17163, revealing prominent low-excitation metal lines and hydrogen recombination features, but lacking the pronounced CO absorption seen in IRC+10420. The MATISSE observations reveal the first high angular scales of the source in the L-band and spatially resolve the Brα line-emitting region, which hints at a marginally asymmetric and variable ionised wind. FORS2 spectropolarimetry points to deviations from perfect spherical symmetry also on larger scales. The data show no evidence for a binary companion within the explored parameter space, indicating that the observed clumpy and time-variable mass loss is likely intrinsic to the star rather than companion-driven. Our results demonstrate that IRAS 17163 hosts a dense, structured, and time-variable wind, coexisting with extended dusty shells. The comparison with IRC+10420 highlights diversity among post-RSG YHGs. These findings emphasise the role of clumpy and near-symmetric mass-loss in shaping the circumstellar medium of evolved massive stars, with implications for their subsequent evolution and core-collapse supernova progenitor properties.

AI.tldr: Multi-wavelength study of yellow hypergiant IRAS 17163-3907 reveals dense, structured, time-variable wind with extended dusty shells, showing clumpy near-symmetric mass loss without binary influence.
AI.motivation: To investigate mass-loss history, geometry, and physical conditions of yellow hypergiant IRAS 17163-3907 in post-RSG stage, placing it in context with IRC+10420, to understand how mass-loss processes influence type II supernova progenitors (25-40 Msun stars).
AI.method: Combined M-band high-resolution CRIRES+ spectroscopy, VLTI/MATISSE mid-infrared L-band interferometry, and FORS2 optical spectropolarimetry to probe both small-scale circumstellar structure and large-scale dusty environment.
AI.result: First M-band coverage shows low-excitation metal lines and hydrogen recombination features without pronounced CO absorption; MATISSE resolves Brα line-emitting region showing marginally asymmetric/variable ionized wind; FORS2 indicates deviations from spherical symmetry; no binary companion detected; evidence for dense, structured, time-variable wind coexisting with extended dusty shells.
AI.conclusion: IRAS 17163 hosts clumpy, near-symmetric mass loss intrinsic to the star, highlighting diversity among post-RSG yellow hypergiants and emphasizing role of such mass-loss processes in shaping circumstellar medium of evolved massive stars with implications for supernova progenitor properties.

title: Bolometric corrections of stellar oscillation mode amplitudes as observed by the PLATO mission. I. Planck-spectrum estimates
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures, accepted for publication in A&A
summary:
We derive bolometric correction functions for oscillation mode amplitudes observed by the different cameras of the ESA PLATO mission. Such corrections between bolometric (full light) and mission instrument-specific amplitudes enable comparisons to theoretical expectations and amplitude conversion between different photometric missions, which is essential for proper detectability yields and target selection.
  Bolometric correction functions were calculated assuming a Planck function approximation for the stellar spectral flux distribution. The calculations follow the procedures applied in earlier analyses for the NASA Kepler and TESS missions. We derived power-law and polynomial parametrisations of the bolometric corrections with $T_{\rm eff}$.
  We find that on average, oscillation mode amplitudes from PLATO's normal cameras (N-CAMs) are expected to be ~6.7% lower compared to Kepler, and ~12.5% higher compared to TESS. A significant average amplitude ratio of ~25% is expected for amplitudes measured using the blue PLATO fast camera (F-CAM) compared to TESS. We find that observations of bright solar-like oscillators, especially with PLATO's F-CAMs, would provide an important test of the predicted corrections.

AI.tldr: The paper derives bolometric correction functions for oscillation mode amplitudes observed by PLATO mission cameras, enabling amplitude comparisons between different photometric missions and theoretical predictions.
AI.motivation: To enable comparisons between theoretical expectations and mission-specific amplitude measurements, and to facilitate amplitude conversion between different photometric missions (essential for proper detectability yields and target selection).
AI.method: Calculated bolometric correction functions using Planck function approximation for stellar spectral flux distribution, following procedures from earlier Kepler and TESS analyses. Derived power-law and polynomial parametrizations with effective temperature (Teff).
AI.result: PLATO's normal cameras (N-CAMs) show ~6.7% lower amplitudes than Kepler and ~12.5% higher than TESS. PLATO's blue fast camera (F-CAM) shows ~25% higher amplitudes compared to TESS. Bright solar-like oscillators observed with F-CAMs would test the corrections.
AI.conclusion: Bolometric correction functions for PLATO mission cameras have been derived, providing essential tools for amplitude comparisons across missions and theoretical validation, with F-CAM observations offering important validation opportunities.

title: ZTF J021804.16+071152.93: a dead cataclysmic variable and potential solution to the missing period bouncers
comment: 13 pages, 8 figures, 2 tables. Accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: It is predicted that half or more of all cataclysmic variables (CVs) should have evolved past the period minimum and now exist as so-called "period bouncers" where a white dwarf should be accreting from a Roche-lobe filling substellar companion. However, this prediction stands in stark contrast to observations, where only a few per cent of CVs are found in this evolutionary phase. A potential solution to this discrepancy is that a magnetic field emerges from within the white dwarf after the system has reached the period minimum. The transfer of angular momentum from the spin of the white dwarf into the orbit then pushes the two stars apart, detaching them for potentially billions of years. Here we present the discovery of ZTF J021804.16+071152.93, a detached $0.69\pm0.01 M_{\odot}$, 19 MG magnetic white dwarf plus $37\pm5 M_\mathrm{Jup}$ brown dwarf binary with an orbital period of 1.7 hours. The kinematics of the system indicate that it is a high probability member of the galactic thick disk. However, this strongly disagrees with the much younger age of the system obtained from the white dwarf parameters, implying that the system may have been accreting in the past. This system is therefore consistent with having detached as a result of the emergence of the magnetic field of the white dwarf when the system was still mass transferring, and may represent the ultimate fate for many (perhaps even most) CVs.

AI.tldr: Discovery of a detached magnetic white dwarf-brown dwarf binary with 1.7-hour orbital period, providing evidence that magnetic field emergence in white dwarfs can detach cataclysmic variables and potentially explain the missing "period bouncers" in observations.
AI.motivation: To address the discrepancy between theoretical predictions (that half of cataclysmic variables should be "period bouncers" with white dwarfs accreting from substellar companions) and observations (only a few percent found in this phase), suggesting magnetic field emergence in white dwarfs could detach systems and explain the missing population.
AI.method: Discovery and characterization of ZTF J021804.16+071152.93, a detached binary system consisting of a 0.69±0.01 M⊙, 19 MG magnetic white dwarf and a 37±5 MJup brown dwarf companion with 1.7-hour orbital period, using observational data and kinematic analysis.
AI.result: The system shows contradictory age indicators: kinematics suggest membership in the older galactic thick disk, while white dwarf parameters indicate a much younger age, implying past accretion history. This supports the hypothesis that magnetic field emergence detached the system during mass transfer.
AI.conclusion: This system represents observational evidence that magnetic field emergence in white dwarfs can detach cataclysmic variables, potentially explaining the missing population of period bouncers and may represent the ultimate evolutionary fate for many (perhaps most) cataclysmic variables.

title: A simulation-based inference of the Milky Way merger history
summary: Accreted stars in the Milky Way (MW) preserve information about the progenitor galaxies where they formed in their chemical and kinematic properties. In this study, we use the chemo-dynamical signatures in the merger debris to approximate the posterior distribution of disrupted satellite properties at the time of infall. Adopting a simulation-based inference framework, we train an ensemble of normalizing flows using samples of merger debris from the Auriga suite of simulations of MW-like galaxies. Applying this methodology to a local sample of accreted stars in the MW, we infer the lookback times, stellar and halo masses, and halo mass merger ratios of several known accretion events in the Galaxy: Gaia Enceladus-Sausage (GES), Helmi streams, Heracles, I'itoi, LMS-1/Wukong, Sagittarius (Sgr), Sequoia and Thamnos. Our predictions align with the accretion time and mass estimates from the literature, and the expected relation between the progenitor stellar masses and debris metallicities across redshifts. The total stellar mass accreted from these events is predicted to be $2.2^{+1.1}_{-0.6}\times10^{9}~\rm{M_{\odot}}$, with GES and Sgr being the largest contributors. The predicted stellar mass accreted from fully disrupted progenitors in the stellar halo is $1.3^{+1.0}_{-0.5}\times10^{9}~\rm{M_{\odot}}$, which is consistent with previous mass measurements of this component. We provide a prediction for the evolution of the MW halo mass until the accretion of Sgr ($z\approx1$): specifically, we find that the mass growth of the Galaxy from the time of its first merger ($z\approx5$) to $z\approx2$ exceeds the total mass of the known progenitors accreted during that interval, suggesting the presence of unidentified substructures. Our estimate of the Galaxy halo mass after the Sgr merger, but prior to the accretion of the Magellanic Clouds, is $5.9^{+1.4}_{-1.1}\times10^{11}~\rm{M_{\odot}}$.

AI.tldr: Using simulation-based inference with normalizing flows on accreted stars, researchers infer properties of disrupted satellite galaxies in the Milky Way, including accretion times, masses, and halo mass evolution.
AI.motivation: To reconstruct the accretion history of the Milky Way by inferring properties of disrupted satellite galaxies from the chemical and kinematic signatures preserved in accreted stars.
AI.method: Adopted a simulation-based inference framework using normalizing flows trained on merger debris from the Auriga suite of cosmological simulations, then applied to local accreted stars in the Milky Way.
AI.result: Inferred lookback times, stellar/halo masses, and merger ratios for known accretion events (GES, Helmi streams, Heracles, I'itoi, LMS-1/Wukong, Sgr, Sequoia, Thamnos). Total accreted stellar mass: 2.2±0.6×10⁹ M⊙. Predicted MW halo mass after Sgr merger: 5.9±1.1×10¹¹ M⊙. Found evidence for unidentified substructures.
AI.conclusion: The methodology successfully reconstructs Milky Way accretion history, validates previous estimates, provides quantitative constraints on progenitor properties, and suggests missing substructures in the early growth phase.

title: Detection of a molecular hydrogen envelope around nova GK Persei
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 1 table to appear in the ApJ Letters
summary: The eruption of Nova Persei 1901 (GK Per) occurred 125 yrs ago; remarkably it still holds major surprises. Using data from the Spectro-Photometer for the History of the Universe, Epoch of Reionization, and Ices Explorer (SPHEREx), we find it has a bipolar molecular hydrogen shell. This shell, which has dimensions 18'x10', is co-spatial with the Halpha nebulosity surrounding the nova, which is purported to be an ancient planetary nebula (PN). The shell is detected most strongly in the 0--0 S(9) 4.6947 micron line. A filament of emission in the S(9) 4.6947 micron line is seen 45" SW of GKPer. This coincides, over much of its length, with the site of X-ray and non-thermal radio emission where the 1901 nova ejecta impinges on the ambient medium. We propose that the H_2 emission from the filament arises from the predicted neutral zone between the forward and reverse shocks. Since it is common for bipolar PNe to be accompanied by H_2 envelopes, it ostensibly suggests that the 18'x10' nebulosity is a conventional PN with a luminous, ionizing central source. We show this is not the case, and that the H$α$ nebulosity may be surrounding gas belonging to pre-existing material that was ionized during the 1901 eruption. The ionized gas is presently undergoing recombination on a timescale of ~3000 years, explaining why the nebulosity is still visible.

AI.tldr: Discovery of a bipolar molecular hydrogen shell around Nova Persei 1901 (GK Per) using SPHEREx data, challenging previous assumptions about the surrounding Hα nebulosity being an ancient planetary nebula.
AI.motivation: To investigate the nature of the Hα nebulosity surrounding Nova Persei 1901, which was previously thought to be an ancient planetary nebula, using new SPHEREx observations to understand its molecular hydrogen content and origin.
AI.method: Used data from SPHEREx (Spectro-Photometer for the History of the Universe, Epoch of Reionization, and Ices Explorer) to detect molecular hydrogen emission lines, particularly the 0-0 S(9) 4.6947 micron line, and analyzed the spatial distribution of H₂ emission in relation to known Hα, X-ray, and radio features.
AI.result: Found a bipolar molecular hydrogen shell (18'×10') co-spatial with the Hα nebulosity, plus a filament of H₂ emission 45" SW of GK Per coinciding with X-ray and non-thermal radio emission sites. The H₂ filament likely originates from the neutral zone between forward and reverse shocks where nova ejecta interact with ambient medium.
AI.conclusion: The Hα nebulosity is not a conventional planetary nebula but rather pre-existing material that was ionized during the 1901 nova eruption and is currently undergoing recombination on a ~3000-year timescale, explaining its continued visibility.

title: Simulating AGN wind feedback with variable feedback efficiencies in idealised disc galaxies
summary: Active Galactic Nucleus (AGN) feedback plays a critical role in galaxy formation and evolution. AGN-driven winds can significantly influence their host galaxies, although the details of their impact remain unclear. In this study, we investigate the feedback effects of AGN winds on idealized disc galaxies using the SWIFT hydrodynamical code with COLIBRE subgrid physics. We implement a new thermal AGN feedback model in which the energy injection coupling efficiency has a power-law dependence on the Eddington ratio of the black hole (BH) accretion rate, motivated by scaling relations for AGN winds from numerical models and observations. We simulate idealised Milky Way-mass galaxies, incorporating a BH, cold gas disc, stellar disc, and hot circumgalactic medium, within a static dark matter halo. We vary the BH mass and the slope and normalisation of the new coupling efficiency model. For a fixed BH mass, we find that while systematic trends with coupling efficiency exist, most galaxy and BH properties show only modest variations. This likely reflects BH self-regulation in the COLIBRE model, which modulates the effects of changes in the feedback efficiency, provided the BH mass is sufficiently high. Key exceptions are the BH accretion rate and mass growth history, and outflow behaviour, where lower coupling efficiencies lead to faster BH growth and weaker outflows, potentially helping to explain the presence of overmassive BHs at high redshifts. Varying the BH mass, however, has a much larger impact, confirming that BH mass remains the primary factor shaping galaxy and BH evolution in our simulations.

AI.tldr: Researchers implement a new thermal AGN feedback model with power-law coupling efficiency dependent on Eddington ratio in SWIFT hydrodynamical simulations of Milky Way-mass galaxies, finding BH mass has larger impact on galaxy evolution than feedback efficiency variations.
AI.motivation: AGN feedback is critical for galaxy formation but its detailed impact remains unclear, particularly how AGN-driven winds influence host galaxies. The study aims to understand these feedback effects using a new thermal AGN feedback model with physically-motivated coupling efficiency.
AI.method: Used SWIFT hydrodynamical code with COLIBRE subgrid physics to simulate idealized Milky Way-mass galaxies with BH, cold gas disc, stellar disc, and hot circumgalactic medium in static dark matter halo. Implemented new thermal AGN feedback model where energy injection coupling efficiency follows power-law dependence on Eddington ratio of BH accretion rate, motivated by numerical models and observations. Varied BH mass and parameters of coupling efficiency model (slope and normalization).
AI.result: For fixed BH mass, most galaxy and BH properties show only modest variations despite systematic trends with coupling efficiency, likely due to BH self-regulation in COLIBRE model. Key exceptions: BH accretion rate, mass growth history, and outflow behavior - lower coupling efficiencies lead to faster BH growth and weaker outflows, potentially explaining overmassive BHs at high redshifts. Varying BH mass has much larger impact than feedback efficiency variations, confirming BH mass as primary factor shaping galaxy and BH evolution.
AI.conclusion: BH mass remains the dominant factor in shaping galaxy and BH evolution in these simulations, with feedback efficiency variations having more modest effects due to BH self-regulation. However, feedback efficiency variations significantly affect BH growth rates and outflow behavior, providing insights into high-redshift overmassive BH populations.

title: Signatures of Reconnection and a Split Heliospheric Tail in High-Energy Energetic Neutral Atoms
summary: The shape of the heliosphere, regarded as comet-like since the 1960s, has recently been the subject of intense debate in the last decade. There is disagreement whether the heliospheric tail extends to $\sim$10,000 au in a comet-like shape or if it is short ($\sim$400 au) with a split. Energetic neutral atom (ENA) maps from Cassini/INCA at energies from 5.2 to 13.5 keV revealed a global structure extending from the nose to the heliospheric tail known as the Belt whose origin has remained largely unexplored. Here, we use a state-of-the-art multi-ion magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) model and a novel reconnection simulation to establish that the Belt structure is consistent with a split tail heliosphere but not with a comet-like heliosphere. In a split-tail heliosphere there is a region of low-$β$ (ratio of thermal to magnetic pressure) in the downwind direction close to the heliopause. Direct simulations of this region reveal that magnetic reconnection is strong and drives the energetic particles that produce the >5.2keV ENAs measured by INCA in the low latitude portion of the Belt. Since the comet-like heliosphere does not produce this low-$β$ region and the resultant reconnection-drive mechanism for the >5.2keV ENAs, the INCA observations are inconsistent with a comet-like heliosphere. Further, these simulations and analysis establish for the first time that magnetic reconnection in the complex magnetic fields, expected in astrospheres across the universe, are likely to be a source of energetic particles and radiation.

AI.tldr: The paper uses MHD modeling and reconnection simulations to show that Cassini/INCA ENA observations of a "Belt" structure support a split-tail heliosphere model over the traditional comet-like shape, with magnetic reconnection in low-β regions driving the observed energetic particles.
AI.motivation: There is ongoing debate about the heliosphere's shape - whether it has a long comet-like tail (~10,000 au) or a short split tail (~400 au). Cassini/INCA observations revealed a "Belt" structure whose origin was unexplained, and this paper aims to determine which heliospheric model best explains these observations.
AI.method: The authors use a state-of-the-art multi-ion magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) model and novel reconnection simulations to analyze the heliospheric structure. They specifically examine the low-β region near the heliopause in the downwind direction and simulate magnetic reconnection processes in this region.
AI.result: The simulations show that only the split-tail heliosphere model produces a low-β region near the heliopause where strong magnetic reconnection occurs. This reconnection drives energetic particles that produce the >5.2 keV ENAs observed by Cassini/INCA in the Belt structure. The comet-like heliosphere model does not produce this low-β region or the reconnection-driven particle acceleration mechanism.
AI.conclusion: The INCA observations of the Belt structure are inconsistent with a comet-like heliosphere and instead support a split-tail heliosphere model. Additionally, the study establishes that magnetic reconnection in complex astrospheric magnetic fields is likely a universal source of energetic particles and radiation across the universe.

title: SPHEREx Wide-Field Infrared Spectral Mapping of Interstellar Ices and Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons
comment: 23 pages, 13 figures, 1 table. Accepted to the Astrophysical Journal March 11, 2026
summary: We present some of the first infrared spectral maps acquired by SPHEREx. These maps, which to our knowledge are the largest of their type ever compiled in the near-infrared, reveal multiple strong lines due to interstellar ices and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) throughout the Cygnus X and North American Nebula regions. The maps emphasize the strongest features arising from the 3 $μ$m H$_2$O, 4.27 $μ$m CO$_2$, and 4.67 $μ$m CO lines and the 3.28 $μ$m PAH feature, all of which are detected over large areas with complex and filamentary spatial distributions. The ice absorption maps of H$_2$O and CO$_2$ in particular broadly trace dense, cold, and well-shielded regions across Cygnus X, consistent with the established picture of efficient ice formation in dense molecular clouds. The interstellar ice features are also detected abundantly in diffuse absorption over wide areas. The relative strength of the H$_2$O and CO$_2$ features varies among different lines of sight, indicating possible differences in local physical conditions or chemical variations. The 3.28 $μ$m PAH emission correlates with the emission from the 7.7 and 11.2 $μ$m features, but shows small differences that may trace the grain size distribution and variations in the ambient UV field. SPHEREx all-sky spectral imaging, of which only a small fraction is showcased in this work, will support numerous science investigations including the structure of the Galaxy, the physics of the interstellar medium, and the chemistry of stars.

AI.tldr: SPHEREx infrared spectral maps reveal widespread interstellar ice and PAH features in Cygnus X and North American Nebula regions, showing complex spatial distributions and variations in chemical composition.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate SPHEREx's capabilities in infrared spectral mapping and showcase the first large-scale near-infrared maps revealing interstellar ice and PAH distributions, which provide insights into interstellar medium physics and chemistry.
AI.method: Used SPHEREx infrared spectral imaging to create large-scale maps of Cygnus X and North American Nebula regions, focusing on key spectral features: 3 μm H₂O, 4.27 μm CO₂, 4.67 μm CO ice absorption lines, and 3.28 μm PAH emission feature.
AI.result: Detected strong ice and PAH features over large areas with complex filamentary distributions; ice maps trace dense, cold, shielded regions consistent with efficient ice formation in molecular clouds; H₂O and CO₂ relative strengths vary indicating local physical/chemical differences; PAH emission correlates with other PAH features but shows variations possibly related to grain size distribution and UV field.
AI.conclusion: SPHEREx's all-sky spectral imaging capabilities, demonstrated through these initial maps, will enable numerous science investigations into Galactic structure, interstellar medium physics, and stellar chemistry, with only a small fraction of its full potential showcased in this work.

title: HaloFlow II: Robust Galaxy Halo Mass Inference with Domain Adaptation
summary: Precise halo mass ($M_h$) measurements are crucial for cosmology and galaxy formation. HaloFlow introduced a simulation-based inference (SBI) framework that uses state-of-the-art simulated galaxy images to precisely infer $M_h$. However, for HaloFlow to be applied to observations, it must be generalizable even when the underlying galaxy formation physics differ from those in the simulations on which it was trained. Without this generalization, HaloFlow produces biased and overconfident $M_h$ posteriors when applied to simulations with different physics. We introduce HaloFlow$^{\rm DA}$, an extension of HaloFlow that integrates domain adaptation (DA) with SBI to mitigate these cross-simulation shifts. Using synthetic galaxy images forward-modeled from the IllustrisTNG, EAGLE, and SIMBA simulations, we test two DA methods: Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks (DANN) and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD). Incorporating DA significantly reduces bias and improves calibration, with MMD achieving the most stable performance, lowering the normalized residual metric, $β$, by an average of 31% and up to 57% when trained and tested on different simulations. Overall, HaloFlow$^{\rm DA}$ produces more robust, less biased with similar precision, $M_h$ constraints than the standard approach using the stellar-to-halo mass relation. HaloFlow$^{\rm DA}$ enables consistent, simulation-trained inference models to generalize across domains, establishing a foundation for robust $M_h$ inference from real HSC-SSP observations.

AI.tldr: HaloFlowDA integrates domain adaptation with simulation-based inference to improve halo mass measurement generalization across different galaxy formation physics simulations, reducing bias by up to 57%.
AI.motivation: Standard HaloFlow produces biased and overconfident halo mass posteriors when applied to observations with different underlying galaxy formation physics than the training simulations, limiting its practical application to real observational data.
AI.method: Extends HaloFlow with domain adaptation (DA) using two methods: Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks (DANN) and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD). Tests on synthetic galaxy images from IllustrisTNG, EAGLE, and SIMBA simulations to mitigate cross-simulation shifts.
AI.result: DA significantly reduces bias and improves calibration, with MMD achieving most stable performance: lowers normalized residual metric β by average 31% and up to 57% when trained and tested on different simulations. Produces more robust, less biased constraints with similar precision compared to standard stellar-to-halo mass relation approach.
AI.conclusion: HaloFlowDA enables consistent, simulation-trained inference models to generalize across domains, establishing foundation for robust halo mass inference from real HSC-SSP observations by addressing domain shift between training simulations and observational data.

title: Integral Field Spectroscopy of Collisional Ring Galaxies I: Stellar Populations Analysis
comment: 18 pages, 12 figures, accepted to be published in MNRAS
summary: Collisional ring galaxies are produced by the collision of a disk galaxy with a compact galaxy plunging through the disk, forming a ring-shaped expanding density wave, triggering star formation at its wake. The wave expansion is expected to produce negative stellar age gradients in radial profiles of post-collision stellar populations. Integral field spectroscopy combined with stellar population synthesis allows us to spatially resolve the stellar populations, to separate the post-collision and pre-collision components, and to produce the radial profiles. We analyse three candidate galaxies: Arp~143, NGC~2793, and VII~Zw~466. Observations were performed with the Calar Alto 3.5~m~telescope using the PMAS/PPak spectrophotometer. NGC 2793 presents a positive stellar age gradient, dismissing the collision hypothesis. For Arp~143 and VII~Zw~466, we found negative stellar age gradients for the youngest stellar populations, up to the ring radii, consistent with the collision hypothesis. We estimated that the collisions occurred $\sim$300~Myr and $\sim$100~Myr (expansion velocities of 33~$\pm$~10 km s$^{-1}$ and 108~$\pm$~26 km s$^{-1}$), respectively, before the density waves reached the observed ring radii. A spatially resolved analysis of the specific star formation histories (sSFH), reveals an expected star formation enhancement following the collision. The sSFH also allowed to identify the most probable intruder galaxy for VII~Zw~466. We report new redshifts for its group members. Finally, radial profiles of light contributions from pre-collisional and post-collisional stars show that the density wave dragged old pre-collisional stars along, as predicted by simulations.

AI.tldr: Collisional ring galaxies show negative stellar age gradients from expanding density waves; analysis of three candidates confirms collision signatures in two galaxies with estimated collision times of ~300 Myr and ~100 Myr.
AI.motivation: To test the collisional ring galaxy formation hypothesis by analyzing stellar age gradients in candidate galaxies, as predicted by simulations where expanding density waves create negative age gradients from the center outward.
AI.method: Used integral field spectroscopy with PMAS/PPak spectrophotometer on Calar Alto 3.5m telescope to obtain spatially resolved stellar populations. Applied stellar population synthesis to separate post-collision and pre-collision components and create radial profiles. Analyzed specific star formation histories (sSFH) to identify collision signatures and intruder galaxies.
AI.result: NGC 2793 showed positive age gradient, dismissing collision hypothesis. Arp 143 and VII Zw 466 showed negative stellar age gradients for youngest populations up to ring radii, consistent with collisions. Estimated collision times: ~300 Myr (Arp 143, expansion velocity 33±10 km/s) and ~100 Myr (VII Zw 466, expansion velocity 108±26 km/s). sSFH revealed star formation enhancement following collision and identified probable intruder for VII Zw 466. Radial profiles showed density wave dragged old pre-collisional stars.
AI.conclusion: The study confirms collisional origin for Arp 143 and VII Zw 466 through negative stellar age gradients and sSFH analysis, supporting theoretical predictions. NGC 2793 is not a collisional ring galaxy. The method successfully separates collision signatures and provides timing estimates for collision events.

title: Galaxy fly-bys sustain bar-halo friction and bar slowdown in disk galaxies
comment: 11 pages, 10 figures, accepted for publication in PASJ
summary: Bars in disk galaxies slow down as they transfer their angular momentum to their dark matter halo via dynamical friction from near-resonant orbits. This bar-halo dynamical friction can become ineffective once phase mixing erases the phase-space gradient around the main resonances. We present fully self-consistent $N$-body simulations of a Milky Way-like disk galaxy with a single dwarf-galaxy fly-by in prograde and retrograde orbits before, during, and after bar formation. In our models, the fly-bys do not trigger a long-lived tidal bar; the bar forms on essentially the same time as in the isolated model. After the encounter, however, all perturbed models develop bars that are stronger and slower than in the isolated one. The final pattern speed depends little on the encounter time, but it does depend on the encounter direction relative to the disk rotation: prograde encounters slow the bar more than retrograde ones. The angular-momentum evolution shows that the disk loses its angular momentum and the halo gains it, consistent with bar-halo friction. By probing the particle distribution of the halo in angle-action space, we demonstrate that the isolated bar enters a metastable, saturated state with a flattened distribution in the phase space around the bar's corotation resonance, whereas a dwarf passage excites long-lived fluctuations in the halo that restore the phase-space gradients near the corotation and thereby sustain the bar-halo friction. This mechanism explains the continued slowdown and growth of bars after fly-bys. It may be relevant to the Milky Way, whose bar formed near the epoch of a major ancient accretion event, suggesting that an early encounter could have influenced the subsequent secular evolution of the bar.

AI.tldr: Dwarf galaxy fly-bys sustain bar-halo friction by restoring phase-space gradients near corotation, leading to stronger, slower bars than in isolated galaxies.
AI.motivation: To understand how dwarf galaxy fly-bys affect bar evolution in disk galaxies, particularly why bars become stronger and slower after encounters despite bar-halo friction normally saturating in isolated systems.
AI.method: Fully self-consistent N-body simulations of a Milky Way-like disk galaxy with single dwarf-galaxy fly-bys in prograde and retrograde orbits, timed before, during, and after bar formation. Analysis of angular momentum evolution and halo particle distribution in angle-action space.
AI.result: Fly-bys don't trigger tidal bars but produce stronger, slower bars than isolated models. Prograde encounters slow bars more than retrograde ones. Isolated bars reach saturated state with flattened phase-space distribution near corotation, while fly-bys excite long-lived fluctuations that restore phase-space gradients, sustaining bar-halo friction.
AI.conclusion: Dwarf galaxy encounters sustain bar-halo friction by preventing phase-space saturation, explaining continued bar slowdown and growth. This mechanism may be relevant to the Milky Way, whose bar formed near a major ancient accretion event.

title: Beyond the Merger-Quasar-Quench Paradigm I: Mergers are neither necessary nor sufficient to quench central galaxies in IllustrisTNG
comment: Accepted for publication in MNRAS. 31 pages
summary: The cessation of star formation in galaxies, known as 'quenching', is a complex, multi-scale process which has been theorized to be linked to galaxy mergers. In this paper, we investigate the potential role of mergers in quenching galaxies in the IllustrisTNG cosmological hydrodynamical simulation. We track the evolution of over 11,000 central galaxies in the simulation with stellar mass $M_\star \ge 10^9 M_\odot$ at $z = 0$ throughout the entirety of cosmic history. We compare their star formation and merger histories to test whether mergers are necessary or sufficient for inducing quenching in the simulation. Only a very small fraction of mergers (about 3 per cent of major mergers and about 12 per cent of all mergers) lead to quenching within 1 Gyr, indicating that mergers are not sufficient by themselves to cause quenching. Furthermore, the vast majority of quenching events are not preceded by a merger within 1 Gyr. Once random coincidences are accounted for and a stellar mass-matched control sample is applied, no merger excess is observed. Hence, mergers are clearly not necessary for quenching to occur in the simulation. Finally, we perform a series of random forest classification and regression analyses to assess the integrated role of mergers in galaxy quenching and supermassive black hole growth in IllustrisTNG. We determine that secular processes dominate the growth of supermassive black holes and the quenching of central galaxies in this simulation, in stark contrast to prior theoretical expectations from idealized hydrodynamical simulations.

AI.tldr: Mergers are neither necessary nor sufficient for galaxy quenching in IllustrisTNG simulation; secular processes dominate instead.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether galaxy mergers play a significant role in quenching (stopping star formation) in galaxies, testing theoretical expectations from idealized simulations against cosmological simulations.
AI.method: Analyzed over 11,000 central galaxies in IllustrisTNG cosmological hydrodynamical simulation, tracking star formation and merger histories. Used statistical analysis including random coincidences accounting, stellar mass-matched control samples, and random forest classification/regression to assess merger-quenching relationship.
AI.result: Only 3% of major mergers and 12% of all mergers lead to quenching within 1 Gyr; most quenching events not preceded by mergers; no merger excess observed after statistical controls; secular processes dominate black hole growth and quenching.
AI.conclusion: Mergers are neither necessary nor sufficient for galaxy quenching in IllustrisTNG, contradicting prior theoretical expectations and highlighting the dominance of secular processes in cosmological simulations.

title: Chemical Properties and Sagittarius-induced Dynamical Perturbations of the GD-1 Stream
summary: In this study, we investigate the chemical properties of the GD-1 stream using cross-matched, data-driven elemental abundances. The results reveal no clear $α$-knee in the [Mg/Fe]-[Fe/H] plane, and strong abundance consistency between the thin stream and cocoon, supporting a common origin. The absence of multiple-population signatures (e.g., C-N anti-correlation) suggests a low-mass progenitor. Using a test-particle simulation with the particle spray method and including perturbations from the Sagittarius (Sgr) dwarf galaxy, it shows that Sgr does not significantly heat the stream to form the cocoon, but modifies the intrinsic $φ_2$ distribution, in agreement with observations. The trailing arm narrowly distributed across the width of the stream, while the leading arm is more diffuse, indicating that major fraction of cocoon stars are present towards the leading arm. Sgr also drags more stream particles moving toward the Galactic center, producing an excess at $V_{\text{GSR}}<0$, consistent with data. Our study confirms the Sgr has a non-negligible dynamical influence on the GD-1 stream. Other heating mechanisms (e.g., dark matter sub-halo encounters and pre-stripping process inside the parent halo) remain to be considered, and higher-resolution spectroscopy is needed to further constrain chemical abundances.

AI.tldr: GD-1 stellar stream chemical analysis shows no α-knee, consistent abundances between stream and cocoon suggesting common origin, with Sagittarius dwarf galaxy perturbations affecting stream morphology but not heating it significantly.
AI.motivation: To investigate the chemical properties and dynamical evolution of the GD-1 stellar stream, particularly examining the influence of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy on its structure and the relationship between the main stream and its surrounding cocoon.
AI.method: Used cross-matched, data-driven elemental abundances for chemical analysis; employed test-particle simulations with particle spray method including Sagittarius dwarf galaxy perturbations to model dynamical effects on the stream.
AI.result: No clear α-knee in [Mg/Fe]-[Fe/H] plane; strong abundance consistency between thin stream and cocoon supporting common origin; absence of multiple-population signatures suggests low-mass progenitor; Sgr perturbations modify intrinsic φ₂ distribution but don't significantly heat stream to form cocoon; trailing arm narrowly distributed while leading arm more diffuse; Sgr drags more stream particles toward Galactic center producing V_GSR<0 excess.
AI.conclusion: Sagittarius dwarf galaxy has non-negligible dynamical influence on GD-1 stream morphology but is not responsible for cocoon heating; other heating mechanisms need consideration; higher-resolution spectroscopy required for better chemical abundance constraints.

title: Spectroscopical Confirmation and Lens Modeling of a Complex Strong Lensing System Produced by a Close Galaxy Pair at $z_d=0.79$
summary: We report the spectroscopic confirmation and lens modeling of the complex strong-lens system J0233-0205, in which the deflector consists of a pair of galaxies at zd = 0.790 +/- 0.022, the background source lies at zs = 2.160 +/- 0.002, and the circularized Einstein radius is thetaE = 1.680 arcsec +/- 0.003 arcsec. Our lens modeling requires two lens components, namely two elliptical galaxies with Einstein radii of 0.669 arcsec +/- 0.002 arcsec and 0.735 arcsec +/- 0.002 arcsec, respectively, and a projected separation of 0.513 arcsec (about 3.832 kpc), as well as three source components: two disk galaxies separated by 0.4965 arcsec (about 3.712 kpc), plus a point-like component closely aligned with one of the disks. From a joint lensing and stellar-population analysis, we infer a total stellar mass within the critical curve of the lens pair of (1.956 +/- 0.418) x 10^11 solar masses and a total enclosed mass of (1.107 +/- 0.008) x 10^12 solar masses, corresponding to a projected dark-matter fraction of 82 +/- 4%. The stellar masses of the two lens galaxies are (8.548 +/- 2.128) x 10^10 solar masses and (1.525 +/- 0.295) x 10^11 solar masses, implying dark-matter fractions within the z-band effective radius of 57 +/- 11% and 70 +/- 6%, respectively. The small separation of the lens pair, together with its relatively high deflector redshift, makes J0233-0205 a potentially ideal laboratory for probing the mass distribution and dark-matter content of close galaxy pairs. In addition, the two disk galaxies and the associated point-like source make this system valuable for investigating the merger process in the source plane.

AI.tldr: Spectroscopic confirmation and lens modeling of J0233-0205, a complex strong-lens system with two lens galaxies at z=0.790 and three source components at z=2.160, revealing high dark-matter fractions and potential for studying galaxy pairs and merger processes.
AI.motivation: To characterize the complex strong-lens system J0233-0205, which presents a rare opportunity to study the mass distribution and dark-matter content of close galaxy pairs at relatively high redshift, as well as investigate merger processes in the source plane.
AI.method: Spectroscopic confirmation of redshifts, lens modeling requiring two elliptical galaxy components and three source components (two disk galaxies plus a point-like source), and joint lensing and stellar-population analysis to derive mass properties.
AI.result: The system has lens galaxies at z=0.790±0.022 and source at z=2.160±0.002 with Einstein radius θE=1.680±0.003 arcsec. Lens modeling reveals two elliptical galaxies with Einstein radii 0.669±0.002 arcsec and 0.735±0.002 arcsec separated by 0.513 arcsec (3.832 kpc). Total stellar mass within critical curve is (1.956±0.418)×10¹¹ M☉, total enclosed mass is (1.107±0.008)×10¹² M☉, giving projected dark-matter fraction of 82±4%. Individual lens galaxies have dark-matter fractions of 57±11% and 70±6% within effective radius.
AI.conclusion: J0233-0205 is a valuable laboratory for probing mass distribution and dark-matter content of close galaxy pairs due to its small separation and high deflector redshift. The system's multiple source components also make it useful for studying merger processes in the source plane.

title: Gaia GraL X.: The GraL catalogue of gravitationally lensed quasars Matched with \textit{Gaia} data, redshifts, and time delays
summary: Determining the Hubble constant tension requires alternative strategies, and multiply imaged quasars, with their intermediate redshifts, can potentially be used in this regard. We provide a currently complete catalogue of spectroscopically confirmed lensed quasars with ESA/{\it Gaia} astrometry and photometry, as well as redshifts and time delays when available. In addition to the improved astrometry, the catalogue increases the number of lensed quasars by a factor of 1.5 (now 364, of which 277 are doubles and 87 are quads or triples) and significantly increases the number of lensing galaxies detected (now 218), which represents a major step forward. Redshifts are provided for 347 quasars and 188 deflectors. A completely new table of time delays, required for estimates of $H_0$, is presented, with 195 time delays from 73 systems. {\it Gaia} absolute astrometry is sub-milliarcsecond and covers the entire sky. Future {\it Gaia} data releases will provide long-term photometry, which should provide many more time delays. The catalogues as presented here enable machine-learning techniques to be trained and tested and subsequently applied to the {\it Gaia} data releases. Finally, we derive simple but homogeneous models of the 18 quadruply imaged quasars for which images of all four components are presented in {\it Gaia} DR3.}

AI.tldr: A comprehensive catalog of 364 spectroscopically confirmed lensed quasars with Gaia astrometry, photometry, redshifts, time delays, and lens models for 18 quads, enabling H₀ tension studies and machine learning applications.
AI.motivation: To address the Hubble constant tension by providing a complete catalog of multiply imaged quasars with precise Gaia astrometry and photometry, enabling alternative strategies for cosmological parameter estimation.
AI.method: Compilation of spectroscopically confirmed lensed quasars with ESA/Gaia astrometry and photometry, collection of redshifts, time delays, and development of homogeneous lens models for quadruply imaged systems.
AI.result: Catalog contains 364 lensed quasars (277 doubles, 87 quads/triples) with 218 detected lensing galaxies, 347 quasar redshifts, 188 deflector redshifts, and 195 time delays from 73 systems; includes homogeneous models for 18 quads.
AI.conclusion: The comprehensive catalog enables machine learning applications on Gaia data and provides essential resources for Hubble constant determination using multiply imaged quasars; future Gaia releases will yield more time delays.

title: Does supernova feedback regulate the star formation rate in dwarf galaxies?
comment: Submitted to MNRAS, comments welcome, 15 pages, 13 figures
summary: Stars form in cold, dense clouds embedded in galactic discs, but whether their formation is primarily regulated by gravitational collapse, turbulence, or stellar feedback remains unclear. Using four high-resolution dwarf galaxy simulations with and without supernova (SN) feedback and magnetic fields, we test how feedback regulates the supply of dense gas and, consequently, the star formation rate (SFR). Although the SFR does increase when SNe are turned off, this increase is only by a factor of a few. Instead, across all models, the theoretical maximum SFR originally proposed by Zuckerman and Palmer, defined as the ratio of the total dense gas mass to its mean free-fall time (${M_{\rm dense}}/{\tff}$), always exceeds the measured SFR by nearly two orders of magnitude. Moreover, the increase of the SFR in the case without SNe is accompanied by a nearly corresponding increase of the total dense gas mass ($M_{\rm dense}$), such that the dense-gas depletion time, $τ\equiv {\rm SFR}/M_{\rm dense}$, decreases by only $\sim 33\%$ in the hydrodynamical case and by about 55\% in the magnetohydrodynamical models. This indicates that SN feedback does not primarily act by slowing the collapse of dense gas, but instead by limiting how much diffuse gas can be converted into dense gas. Our results suggest that the main contribution to the regulation of the SFR, at least in dwarf galaxies, may arise from stabilization by galactic rotation, rather than by SN feedback.

AI.tldr: Supernova feedback in dwarf galaxies primarily regulates star formation by limiting diffuse gas conversion to dense gas rather than slowing dense gas collapse, with galactic rotation potentially being the main regulator.
AI.motivation: To determine whether star formation in dwarf galaxies is primarily regulated by gravitational collapse, turbulence, or stellar feedback, specifically testing how supernova feedback affects the supply of dense gas and star formation rates.
AI.method: Used four high-resolution dwarf galaxy simulations comparing models with and without supernova feedback and magnetic fields, analyzing the relationship between dense gas mass, free-fall times, and star formation rates.
AI.result: Turning off SN feedback increased SFR only by a factor of a few; the theoretical maximum SFR (dense gas mass/free-fall time) exceeded actual SFR by nearly two orders of magnitude; SN feedback mainly limits diffuse-to-dense gas conversion rather than slowing dense gas collapse.
AI.conclusion: Supernova feedback does not primarily regulate star formation by slowing dense gas collapse but by limiting diffuse gas conversion to dense gas, suggesting galactic rotation may be the main regulator in dwarf galaxies.

title: Hub Formation and Filament-Filament Collision: An Analytical Model
comment: 10 pages and 9 figures. Publications of the Astronomical Society of Japan in press
summary: Filaments are ubiquitous throughout the Galaxy. Massive star formation is often observed in hub-filament systems, where multiple filaments appear to be interconnected and merging. Filament-filament collisions are therefore a likely triggering mechanism for massive star formation. We derive basic physical properties of filament-filament collisions, such as the collision cross section (CCS), the hub mass, and its mass function, based on a simple cylindrical filament model. We assume a cylindrical filament with length $2p$, full width $2q$, and line-mass $λ_0$, and consider the CCS between two identical filaments. The collision is specified by three vectors: the directions of the colliding filaments ($n_1$ and $n_2$) and the direction of the relative velocity between the two filaments ($n_v=v/|v|$). For the thin filament, $p\gg q$, the CCS is expressed as $S=4p^2|n'_1\times n'_2|$, where $n'_1$ and $n'_2$ represent the directional vectors projected onto a plane perpendicular to the relative velocity $n_v$. As the angle between $n'_1$ and $n'_2$ becomes smaller, the cross section proportional to $p\cdot q$ becomes relatively important. We propose a simple model in which the hub mass is estimated by the overlapping portion of the two colliding filaments. The hub mass function is derived using the CCSs and the geometrically estimated overlapping mass. When the directions and relative velocities of the filaments are isotropically distributed, the mass function expected from a single species of filaments fits well to a power law and the power exponent is $γ_M\simeq -2.96$ ~ $-3.78$. The power exponent of the global hub mass function is the same as that of the line-mass distribution function, $γ_λ\simeq -1.5$. This means that a massive hub is formed by the collision of two massive filaments.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a theoretical model for filament-filament collisions in hub-filament systems, deriving collision cross sections, hub mass estimates, and mass functions based on cylindrical filament geometry.
AI.motivation: Massive star formation often occurs in hub-filament systems where filaments appear interconnected and merging. Filament-filament collisions are a likely triggering mechanism for massive star formation, but their basic physical properties need quantitative derivation.
AI.method: Develops a simple cylindrical filament model with length 2p, width 2q, and line-mass λ₀. Derives collision cross sections for two identical filaments considering their directional vectors (n₁, n₂) and relative velocity direction (n_v). For thin filaments (p≫q), the cross section is S=4p²|n₁'×n₂'| where n₁' and n₂' are projections onto a plane perpendicular to n_v. Estimates hub mass from overlapping portions of colliding filaments and derives hub mass function using cross sections and overlapping mass.
AI.result: For isotropically distributed filament directions and relative velocities, the mass function from a single filament species fits a power law with exponent γ_M ≈ -2.96 to -3.78. The global hub mass function power exponent equals that of the line-mass distribution function (γ_λ ≈ -1.5), indicating massive hubs form from collisions of massive filaments.
AI.conclusion: Filament-filament collisions provide a quantitative framework for understanding massive star formation in hub-filament systems. The derived relationships show that massive hubs naturally arise from collisions of massive filaments, with mass functions following predictable power laws based on filament properties.

title: A near field guide to Roman's wide-area surveys
comment: 26 pages, 13 figures, 8 tables. Submitted to ApJS. Data and code used to generate figures and tables are available at http://github.com/Dynamics-Penn/roman-nearfield
summary: The Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope currently plans to survey nearly 6000 square degrees of the sky, mainly in the High-Latitude Wide-Area Survey (HLWAS) and Galactic Plane Survey (GPS). Although these surveys are optimized for other science, they are also a treasure trove for studying the nearby universe. The foreground of the HLWAS includes 59 known stellar streams, 14 known satellite galaxies, and 9 globular clusters in the Milky Way, and an additional 63 galaxies within 10 Mpc spanning several orders of magnitude in stellar mass. The GPS includes an additional 38 globular clusters in its footprint. We summarize and visualize these populations and discuss some of the relevant characteristics of the planned Roman observations. We also examine the expected astrometric performance of the core surveys based on the anticipated time-baselines between observations, and point out the substantial improvement provided by longer time intervals between repeat observations. In particular, the plan for a 6-month revisit timescale in the HLWAS is a missed opportunity from the perspective of proper motions. These data will nonetheless be a powerful new resource for studying the Milky Way and its neighborhood.

AI.tldr: Roman Space Telescope surveys will serendipitously cover numerous Milky Way structures and nearby galaxies, providing valuable data for local universe studies despite suboptimal proper motion measurements due to short revisit intervals.
AI.motivation: The Roman Space Telescope's planned surveys, while optimized for other science goals, will incidentally cover significant populations of nearby astronomical objects including stellar streams, satellite galaxies, globular clusters, and nearby galaxies. These serendipitous observations represent a valuable opportunity to study the Milky Way and local universe.
AI.method: The paper summarizes and visualizes known astronomical populations within the survey footprints, analyzes the expected astrometric performance based on planned observation time-baselines, and evaluates the impact of revisit intervals on proper motion measurements.
AI.result: The High-Latitude Wide-Area Survey will cover 59 stellar streams, 14 satellite galaxies, 9 globular clusters, and 63 galaxies within 10 Mpc. The Galactic Plane Survey adds 38 more globular clusters. Astrometric analysis shows that longer time intervals between repeat observations would substantially improve proper motion measurements, with the planned 6-month revisit timescale representing a missed opportunity for optimal proper motion science.
AI.conclusion: Despite suboptimal revisit intervals for proper motion measurements, the Roman Space Telescope surveys will provide a powerful new resource for studying the Milky Way and nearby universe, covering diverse populations of astronomical objects that were not the primary survey targets but offer significant scientific value.

title: Are supernovae driving turbulence in the solar neighborhood?
summary: Turbulence plays an important role in shaping the interstellar medium, and strongly influences star formation. We aim to identify the physical processes capable of sustaining HI turbulence in the solar neighborhood. We compare recent HI line-of-sight velocity observations within a volume of radius 70-500 pc centered on the Sun with a suite of 1 kpc numerical simulations that include two distinct turbulent drivers: (i) supernova (SN) feedback and (ii) imposed large-scale turbulent forcing. For each simulation, we construct synthetic sky maps that closely mimic the observational one, allowing for a consistent comparison between the simulations and the observational data. HI observations show a median velocity dispersion of 11.1 km s-1 in the solar neighborhood. SN-driven simulations systematically underpredict this value, yielding dispersions in the range 4.9-6.7 km s-1. Simulations with strong enough large-scale forcing can reproduce not only the median observed velocity dispersion, but also the observed velocity distribution.

AI.tldr: The paper compares HI turbulence observations in the solar neighborhood with simulations using supernova feedback vs. large-scale turbulent forcing, finding that SN-driven models underpredict observed velocity dispersions while large-scale forcing can reproduce both the median dispersion and distribution.
AI.motivation: To identify the physical processes capable of sustaining HI turbulence in the solar neighborhood by comparing observational data with numerical simulations of different turbulent drivers.
AI.method: Compare recent HI line-of-sight velocity observations within 70-500 pc radius centered on the Sun with 1 kpc numerical simulations including two turbulent drivers: (1) supernova feedback and (2) imposed large-scale turbulent forcing. Construct synthetic sky maps from simulations that mimic observational data for consistent comparison.
AI.result: HI observations show median velocity dispersion of 11.1 km s⁻¹. SN-driven simulations systematically underpredict this value (4.9-6.7 km s⁻¹). Simulations with strong enough large-scale forcing can reproduce both the median observed velocity dispersion and the observed velocity distribution.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale turbulent forcing, rather than localized supernova feedback alone, appears necessary to sustain the observed level of HI turbulence in the solar neighborhood, suggesting different physical processes dominate interstellar medium turbulence in this region.

title: Calibrating Galaxy Infall Times in Groups and Clusters with IllustrisTNG Simulations
comment: 15 pages, 12 figures, accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: The time since a galaxy first became a satellite is central to understanding how environment drives galaxy evolution, yet it cannot be measured directly. Using the TNG300 and TNG-Cluster simulations, we track satellites from $z=1$ to $z=0$ and derive a simple, redshift-dependent prescription for ${T}_{\rm{inf}}$ based on position in projected phase space and stellar mass, via symbolic regression. The resulting calibration provides continuous, observation-ready estimates of infall time across projected phase space. In projected phase space, ${T}_{\rm{inf}}$ is often well described by two components, and we provide analytic expressions for the corresponding characteristic timescales. This framework can be applied directly to spectroscopic samples to infer environmental histories in galaxy groups and clusters.

AI.tldr: Researchers develop a method to estimate satellite galaxy infall times using simulations and symbolic regression, creating a calibration based on projected phase space position and stellar mass.
AI.motivation: The time since a galaxy became a satellite is crucial for understanding environmental effects on galaxy evolution, but this infall time cannot be measured directly from observations.
AI.method: Using TNG300 and TNG-Cluster simulations, researchers track satellites from z=1 to z=0 and apply symbolic regression to derive a redshift-dependent prescription for infall time based on position in projected phase space and stellar mass.
AI.result: The calibration provides continuous, observation-ready estimates of infall time across projected phase space, with infall time often well described by two components for which analytic expressions are provided.
AI.conclusion: This framework can be directly applied to spectroscopic samples to infer environmental histories in galaxy groups and clusters, enabling better understanding of how environment drives galaxy evolution.

title: Structural Parameters of the Globular Cluster M 15
summary: We present a detailed analysis of the structural parameters of the globular cluster M 15 using g- and i-band photometric data from Pan-STARRS1 DR2. The central coordinates ($X_{\rm{C}}, Y_{\rm{C}}$), ellipticity ($ε$), and position angle (PA) are derived via two independent methods: ellipse fitting of the two-dimensional stellar number isodensity distribution and Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling. Our analysis of 38 stellar density intervals reveals a cluster center offset by only $4\,.\!\!^{\prime\prime}1\pm9\,.\!\!^{\prime\prime}6$ from the commonly accepted in the literature value, a good agreement on the order of our map resolution. We find a radial variation in the ellipticity, with a mean value of $ε=0.09\pm0.02$ for the inner region ($R\leq4\,.\!\!^{\prime}5$) and $ε=0.04\pm0.02$ for the outer region ($R>4\,.\!\!^{\prime}5$), where the errors correspond to $1σ$. The MCMC analysis of 75 datasets yields a mean $ε=0.022\pm0.005$ for the entire cluster. The PA remains constant with increasing distance from the cluster center, $\rm{PA}=44\,.\!\!{\rm{^\circ}}4\pm16\,.\!\!{\rm{^\circ}}2$, and the MCMC method providing a consistent value of $\rm{PA}=46\,.\!\!{\rm{^\circ}}6\pm7\,.\!\!{\rm{^\circ}}1$. Our results are in agreement with some recent studies but challenge others, suggesting that a single $ε$ value may be insufficient to fully characterize the overall oblateness of M 15 due to incompleteness and crowding effects in its core.

AI.tldr: Analysis of M15 globular cluster structure using Pan-STARRS1 data reveals radial ellipticity variation, constant position angle, and minor center offset from literature values.
AI.motivation: To characterize the structural parameters (center coordinates, ellipticity, position angle) of globular cluster M15 using modern photometric data, addressing discrepancies in previous studies and investigating radial variations in cluster morphology.
AI.method: Used g- and i-band photometric data from Pan-STARRS1 DR2. Applied two independent methods: 1) ellipse fitting of 2D stellar number isodensity distribution across 38 density intervals, and 2) Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling of 75 datasets.
AI.result: Cluster center offset only 4.1±9.6 arcsec from literature value. Radial ellipticity variation: ε=0.09±0.02 for inner region (R≤4.5'), ε=0.04±0.02 for outer region (R>4.5'). MCMC gives mean ε=0.022±0.005 for entire cluster. Constant PA=44.4±16.2° (ellipse fitting) and PA=46.6±7.1° (MCMC).
AI.conclusion: Results agree with some recent studies but challenge others, indicating that a single ellipticity value may be insufficient to characterize M15's overall oblateness due to incompleteness and crowding effects in the core region.

title: Euclid preparation. Far-infrared predictions for Euclid galaxy catalogues: cluster, protocluster, and field
summary: The MAMBO mock galaxy catalogue, based on the Millennium Simulation with empirically assigned galaxy properties, provides predictions of FIR fluxes and physical parameters of Euclid-detectable galaxies. Predicted FIR flux distributions confirm that only the brightest Euclid sources will be detectable in existing FIR surveys. We employ stacking to measure the mean dust properties as a function of stellar mass and redshift. We find dust temperatures and infrared luminosities increase with redshift across all mass bins, while dust masses remain roughly constant. FIR number counts from MAMBO show overall good agreement with observations, and the total infrared luminosity function reproduces published estimates across most redshift ranges, extending to z~10. Comparing the Euclid Wide and Deep Surveys, we find that the EDS recovers the total IRLF to fainter luminosities and higher redshifts (up to z~6 in $I_E$), although its detectability falls below 80% at z>4, whereas the EWS becomes strongly incomplete beyond z~2. We also examine the dependence of the IRLF on environment. Schechter fits indicate that the faint-end slope $α$ flattens with redshift for cluster and protocluster galaxies, while remaining approximately constant for field populations. Imposing additional detection limits from Herschel-PACS and SPIRE shows that only the most luminous ($L_{IR}$ > $10^{12.5}$ $L_{\odot}$) galaxies remain detectable at z~4, but the limited MAMBO area (3.14$deg^2$) is inadequate for statistically robust (>3$σ$) constraints. Survey areas at least 30 times larger are required. Overall, the MAMBO FIR extension reproduces key number count and IRLF trends, provides realistic predictions for FIR-detected Euclid galaxies, and highlights the importance of synergies with current and future FIR/sub-mm facilities to probe environmental dependence with sufficient depth and area.

AI.tldr: MAMBO mock galaxy catalogue predicts FIR properties of Euclid-detectable galaxies, showing only brightest sources will be FIR-detectable, with dust temperatures/LIR increasing with redshift while dust mass stays constant. IRLF reproduces observations to z~10, with Deep Survey better than Wide Survey at high-z. Environmental dependence shows flattening faint-end slope for clusters with redshift.
AI.motivation: To provide realistic predictions for FIR properties of galaxies detectable by Euclid mission, understand dust evolution across cosmic time, and assess synergies between Euclid and FIR/sub-mm facilities for probing environmental dependence of galaxy properties.
AI.method: Uses MAMBO mock galaxy catalogue based on Millennium Simulation with empirically assigned galaxy properties. Employs stacking to measure mean dust properties vs. stellar mass/redshift. Compares predicted FIR number counts and total infrared luminosity function (IRLF) with observations. Analyzes Euclid Wide vs. Deep Survey detectability. Examines IRLF environmental dependence using Schechter fits for field, cluster, and protocluster galaxies.
AI.result: Only brightest Euclid sources detectable in existing FIR surveys. Dust temperatures and infrared luminosities increase with redshift across all mass bins while dust masses remain roughly constant. FIR number counts show good agreement with observations. IRLF reproduces published estimates to z~10. Euclid Deep Survey recovers IRLF to fainter luminosities and higher redshifts (z~6) than Wide Survey (incomplete beyond z~2). Faint-end slope α flattens with redshift for cluster/protocluster galaxies but remains constant for field populations. Herschel-PACS/SPIRE limits show only LIR > 10^12.5 L⊙ galaxies detectable at z~4, requiring 30x larger survey area for robust constraints.
AI.conclusion: MAMBO FIR extension successfully reproduces key observational trends and provides realistic predictions for Euclid galaxies. Highlights critical need for synergies between Euclid and current/future FIR/sub-mm facilities to probe environmental dependence with sufficient depth and area, as current MAMBO area is inadequate for statistically robust constraints at high redshift.
